speaker: interviewer
text: OK. At the end of the last reel, one, you were just talking about the invasion in Poland. OK? Let me ask you. You, you said we ran out to a farm. Who's the we?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, my father, mother, and my little sisters and myself. We didn't know where we were going or what we'd do, but there was shooting going on. Also, there were some-- dropping bombs around us. And when we got out of the city already, we, we didn't, we didn't know where to go. Go forwards or backwards or go home. Of course, German troops were all over us. But since we had small kids, they came over, they looked at us. They looked at my-- my mother had a basket. I believe it was food. They looked inside and told her, go home in German. And, and then we were under German occupation. Beginning, we didn't know anything coming. Some of them were just regular human beings. And they were not bothering anybody until the local Germans, the [? folk ?] [? station, ?] start pointing fingers. And a few days after they took over the city, they already had SS troops. And they already gave orders what to do. They, they used to-- they used to take the young man to clean, to clean the streets. They used to also take him in and-- where they have the barns for, for the horses, the Jewish men used to. I was too young for that, but I was a tall fellow, almost the height I am now. And-- but I was-- they didn't bother me. But after a few weeks, my father-- oh, pardon me. Before that, my father went back-- we-- all of us went back to our own home. He opened up the windows and put in a sign, shoemaker. And he start fixing shoes for the German soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to give him marks. They used to pay. And it was still livable. We did not see any thing unusual except we saw different, different Germans, different uniforms. Instead of Polish, you saw the German. They didn't bother anybody yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we find a sign in Polish and German that the city of Mlawa is changed to Mielau. And they got a, a Burgermeister or a mayor of the city was a German. And the city hall was changed to German. And it took maybe six or-- I think September, October. By the end of October, my father had some kind of disagreement with some local men. And they told us, some of the Poles told us-- either they were Germans or Poles-- you better watch out. They're going to come to arrest you tonight. He had-- my father bought a horse and a small wagon before the war. I don't-- it looks like my father knew already or prepared himself that something is going to happen. We didn't know about it as kids. We were happy that we have a horse. And either he wanted to take away that horse, whatever. Anyways, it was a big disagreement and they were looking for my father. So somebody came over from the neighbors, or I don't know exactly who it was, and told him, you better get out because they're going to come to take you today. And at night, we-- I put on my clothes the way I'm staying now. And my father and we left our house. My mother and the little sisters were staying over there. We went, we walked maybe a half a mile-- half an hour. And we got into a farmer. He probably knew that man because he took us in. He, he, he gave us food. And told him, you stay over here and tomorrow, you going to go home. Unfortunately, the next day we never went home, of course. One of his, brother or somebody who went over there to our house, and they they told him that the Gestapo came at night to look for, for, for my father. Since I was the oldest one, naturally my dad told him, you coming with me. And we stayed over night over there. And the following day or two, we start walking away from Mlawa. And we walked for almost two weeks, mostly at daytime because at night it was against the law to, to, to see civilian on the streets. And once in a while, we used to get a ride from farmers who used go to the market. And the end of October or beginning of November, we got way east, close to the, to the Russian border. It was Poland, but it's the part which the Russian-- the Russian army took over. And we came over there. And at night, at that time, we knew already that there's only one way you can go through the border illegally, it's at night. And I was already knocked out from walking so much, but I was a husky fellow. And we met the first Russian patrol. Nobody speaks Russians. I spoke to them Polish. And somehow they understood me. And I tried to be the interpreter. And they spoke Russian. But somehow we communicated with our hands. And we told him that we are Jews. And they told them, go down over there. So they kept us over there near the border, on the Russian side already. There were no German patrols close to the border. We saw them before but not at the border. They didn't care if somebody wants to go to over on the other side to the Soviet Union. And they, they took us over there. There was a woman. She spoke to us. I think she was some kind of military order integration. No, she was in-- the woman talked to my father. But he couldn't understand, so I answered for him. And she was surprised that I understand what she's saying. And they told her, go straight ahead. And you get to the city and you go to City Hall. And we arrived at Bialystok, in the city-- in the of Bialystok. And that started off almost like a chapter in our life. We never saw my mother again, or my sisters, or anybody else, or uncles or anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you something. I want to take you back a little bit for a little more detail. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you can give it to me. When in, Mlawa--

speaker: interviewee
text: Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, in Mlawa.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any markings? Were there-- were the Germans making any differentiation between the Jews and the rest of the Christians, the Christians in Poland? Did you have any-- have to wear any specific marking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beginning, they didn't do it. But when we are over there, they didn't have any yellow stars yet. It was not-- we didn't know about it. The local population used to point out, this is a Juden. He lives over here. But they did not bother us the first few weeks in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about school at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: School was finished. I never went to school back or my sisters went to school or [NON-ENGLISH]. It. That was-- it was closed, period. It was taken over by the government. Either they had-- they had some kind of people over there, but no kids went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just Jewish kids or all kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kids. Now, the other schools, I don't know what happens because I never went over there. But our school where I used to go was closed and nobody would dare even to go over there because we always saw German, German soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when a Polish person, a Polish Christian person, would point out to this is a Juden, would anything happen? Did you see any kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I'll hear them. Sometimes they just, they just walk away. They don't pay any attention. The first few weeks, it was not too bad at all. I mean, was little difference between the war in, in, in before the Polish government and the German governments. Except ourselves, we were a little bit start to be frightened. Why, I don't know. We never saw anything yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the Germans-- [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: --arrived in Mlawa, Mlawa, I'm sorry. [CLEARS THROAT] You were still young, so did they march in with their shiny boots or how-- Oh, yes. Motorcycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When they came in, first of all, we saw light tanks got in the city, in the center. After that, we saw some very big, heavy Germans on motorcycles. There were two or three, depends on the motorcycles. And they took over some of the nicest homes over there. They, they put on their headquarters. They also took over-- there were big farmers. They had a lot of wheat and corn. They, right away, put up soldiers over there. They should not move it. But there was no fighting in the city. The only fighting was before. On the first day of the, of the war, September 1st, they were fighting. We could hear artillery fire. We heard machine fire. We could hear-- once [INAUDIBLE] was almost dropped on it because it looks like a machine gun fire went through, close by, while we were over there. But otherwise, we didn't see any war. We saw already-- there were already some casualties that we saw. We saw some German soldiers. And also, they picked them up right away. And there was some Polish soldiers that laying there dead in the street. And after a while, we saw the transfer of Polish soldiers as prisoners of war. We used to see big German cars, and inside was soldiers. That was the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you and your father made this journey east across Poland, I was wondering if you could tell me, you said you traveled during the day. But were the roads crowded with people? Were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was such a mix up that the Germans didn't even bother because some went east. Some was going west. And they did not say-- all they look to see if, if you have a uniform. If you had anything it will remind you of a uniform or something close, they will stop him. Otherwise, when you saw a young boy or girl and a man, they did not bother yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about, as you were making this journey east, were there any kind of low flying planes? Was there strafing? Was there any kind of-- were you ever in danger of losing your life due to any kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The first few days when we escaped from Mlawa, there were shootings where planes were-- because we went through a line where it was a wooded area. And there were German soldiers on one side and Polish soldiers on the other side. And we walked through the main highway. We didn't know about it. And somebody took a shot. I don't know who it was. Was it German side or Polish side, in the air it looks like. Because if they would hit us, nobody would come out. And we went through. But we saw from a distance that when you see a man and a woman and children, somehow they still did not bother to let you go through. Then when we got closer, we saw the Germans with machine guns, those heavy machine guns, holding it like that. But they didn't bother us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there communication? Were you able to-- on this journey that you were making east, was there any kind of letter writing to your mother and your sisters? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. No way. The only thing, my dad wrote something and he gave it to the farmer who kept us overnight. And that's the last time. [CRYING] I'm sorry. That's last time I saw-- I saw my mother and my sisters. It's when we left there, the city. Until 1941, we never saw them again. And then when we came on the-- [CRYING] --on the Soviet side, we got in, in, in, in the city, Bialystok, where we find an apartment over there. And my father start opened up a store because people needed repairs. It wasn't a store that he's going to sell anything, but just the repairs. A shoemaker. It really helped a lot because everybody needed repairs. And then some Russian soldiers used to come over, fix it. And that's the way we started off living on the Soviet side. But as far as my parents, my mother and sisters, that's the last time I saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you-- obviously Russian was not a language either you or your father were acquainted with at the time, how did you even find-- you had money, did you have-- could you take lodging somewhere in order to? How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I mean, but my father somehow managed to find a place. He make a deal with somebody that they open up. They needed people to accommodate the public, I mean. And being a shoemaker, it was very, very important. If he would be something else, he could not do it. And we-- I think there were also a lot of Jewish families in Bialystok. And they used to call us [RUSSIAN] or a displaced person. And they used to help out. And that's the way we started off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, while you were in Bialystok, this was when exactly? Do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already in November of '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that point, were you able to correspond with your mother? Was your father able to let her know where you were? Did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In '39, we could not. But by 1940, my father wrote the first letter. And it was delivered to her by official mail from the Soviet Union to Germany. It went to Berlin and then went to, to Mlawa. I started off going back to school. I, I went into the Russian school and started to learn the Russian language. So I-- suddenly, I became an interpreter. And-- not interpreter, I mean. For my father, for the-- there were a lot of refugees, I mean. They escaped from Poland, but mostly they came by themselves. Wives and small children, they were left on the German side. I started school. It was really rough on me because I didn't understand a lot of things and I had to guess. But somehow I managed. And from November till, till '41, I was in school leading to normal life to a certain degree but I was missing my mother and my sisters. My father was-- it was terrible with him. He could not-- he could not take it, I mean, that he left his wife and children. And at one point, he wanted to go back to, to Mlawa. There was a registration in Bialystok that whoever wants to register can register and go back and bring the families. But it was a trick. My father did not register. He had somebody else. And he had a-- one of his friends over there. And he had registered himself to go back to Poland to bring his family. And in the middle of the night, they encircled the whole city. They had the list of every, every person who wants to go back to, to Germany or Poland and the rest of them and they took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: You need to be clear when you talk about they. Who took-- who took them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian army or the [NON-ENGLISH], or the secret police, or whatever they call it. I don't know. They arrested those people at night. They put them in a big, big cattle trains The-- and they all wind up in Siberia. Because they said, if you want to go back to Germany, you are a spy. That was the policy. But fortunately enough for me, my father did not register because he had-- he had a feeling or he knew something which he didn't tell me, that he cannot do it. But in 1940, he, he got contact with my mother and sisters. And he worked with the local government and tried to get a passport that they should come over from Germany to Poland. Because those two years before the war, the 1941 war started, it was normal relation between Germany and Poland. And my mother received the passport with the kids to come to us. And she was over there in, in Mlawa. But later on, she was transferred to a camp already. They organized a camp. And she wrote us, we have the passport, hopefully we're going to see you soon. But they never let those people out. They gave them the passport. They prepared themselves. And they never let them go. And unfortunately, in 19-- later on, I mean, you find out from different people that all those young women with their children were the first one to go to Treblinka. But during-- from 1939 till '41, we got a few letters from Germany-- from Poland or Germany, whatever you want to call it. And she was miserable, for the whole family. For me, as a young boy and naturally I still was in school. And I wasn't miserable but I was not happy either, I mean, because I know my sisters, my mother is over there, and the uncles and everybody else. [CLEARS THROAT] And then, 1941, in June, the Germans attacked us and we were again on the-- the Germans came in to Bialystok. And the fighting started off. And one of the Germans threw in a grenade [INAUDIBLE] my father in the house. And somehow they had big ovens, and I was behind. It's almost like a square box, the ovens, and I was behind over there. And my father was in front of me. And the grenade hit him and it saved my life. And-- He was very-- he was injured very badly. He was dying. After the house exploded-- not the house. It was-- the house went on fire suddenly. And there were a whole row, all homes which went-- had the same problems. Some of the people did not go out at all. They were burnt in the homes. In, in my case, my father was badly injured and I was not injured. And I pulled him out from the house. The house burned down to pieces. And I was-- there was a field next to it, like you're growing potatoes or whatever. And I pulled him in between two lines where they plant the potatoes. And the German went through once or so. It was burning the street. The hounds-- the whole thing over there, the whole area. They left us. And I pulled him to the front. And then they took him to the hospital. And they told me, you go you go whatever you want. We'll let you know. Next day, I find out my, my father was dead in the hospital from the injuries. There were some older people, Jewish people, who came with me. And we took him to the Jewish cemetery. And I was present when he was buried. Now, I was left alone. I didn't know what to do. But a family took me. [CRYING] A family took me in. They told me, you can stay with us. And they had-- they were selling corn, wheat, I mean farm products. And I stayed with them till about-- that was June till about October. But since I was already a grown up man in, in the German's eyes, I had to go-- they used to take me to, to work. But it used to be a school was a headquarters from the German army. And we did all kinds of jobs, cleaning toilets, streets. Anything. They needed help, they had Jewish commanders. And, and I was over there for a while. Then I was transferred to Baranowitsche with, with a group of men. Because the German army was going east, but the highways were so bad that they used to use slave labor, human labor, actually. We were Jews and also they had prisoners of war already, that they used to cut the trees and lay it down parallel and and build the highway so the, so the heavy trucks could go through. And you know, it's almost built on a [INAUDIBLE] as a highway. You know, there's plenty-- it's wooded area those streets, those highways it used to go through. So we used to cut that tree on one side and on the other side to, lay it down to each other. Then you would put in dirt on top so the trucks could go through. And there were a lot of prisoners over there, Soviet prisoners or Russian prisoners. And also in, in the area were also a lot of dead people laying because they were fighting before over there with the Russian troops. And also there were big holes from bombs which exploded. And they used to-- we used to-- we had to take some of the human beings and pull them. [SOBBING] We used to pull those soldiers, dead soldiers, put them in in the big holes and cover them up. And then we had to go back to work. They kept this for, for quite a while until we come to the main highways. This was a side highways because the traffic was so heavy with, with trucks and tanks and whatever you want. And the railroad were very bad, were not modern. They were way behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everything was going to the front going east. You could even hear still the artillery fire. If it was Soviet fire or German fire, I don't know. But you could hear the explosions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or-- then-- we used to get up early in the morning, very little food. Got a piece of bread, and worked all day long. And if you wanted water, you walk away, you walk in the wood, there's no question that you get shot automatically.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they always kept an eye on us. On us and there was in front of us was maybe 100 feet, 200 feet, were prisoners of war. And they treated them the same way.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, they watched them more than they watched the civilian men. Because we were just plain civilians. Wore civilian clothes, we have a shirt maybe for weeks. We didn't know what needs to change our shirt. The sanitary condition was unbelievable. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say it's unbelievable, what does that-- can you explain to me what is unbelievable?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, living in the United States, you compare how we survived, that no showers, no toilets, no basic-- let's say a toothbrush or anything else a human being needs-- were not available. We just living worse than-- worse than animals. Food was very scarce. We get a little food. Is enough maybe for a baby, we used to get for a grown up man and we had to work heavy labor. And-- [CLEARS THROAT] Until-- it took us a few weeks, we completed the project. It took us back to, to the camp. It was not a concentration camp. It was a labor camp in Baranowitsche. And we start-- they started to expand the railroad station. But we had no equipment, machinery, or something else. Everything had to be done by hand. They had railroad truck and there were small, small cars on top. And we used to by hand, pick up the sand, and throw it in. And that's the way we worked 12, 14, 16 hours a day. But the worst thing was until now, until that point, we had to pull those human beings, soldiers-- put in-- we had blood on our hands. Threw them in-- [CRYING] --in those ditches, or, or those big holes. Mostly they tried to use those holes which were bombs for exploded. When a bomb exploded, there's a hole maybe 50, 75, to 100-feet deep. Used to throw it in. Just, just, just like dirt, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, these were Russian or German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were Russian soldiers. The dead soldiers were Russian. The Germans, dead soldiers or anything else, we never saw because they picked them up themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have one last question before we change the reel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living-- from the time your father was killed, where did you live? This is what we'll pick up where we begin the next reel.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [CLEARS THROAT]
